2025/speaker-04.name: Prof. Christian Happi
2025/speaker-04.designation: Professor of Molecular Biology and Genomics Director of Institute of Genomics and Global Health Redeemer’s University, Ede, Nigeria
2025/speaker-04.bodyHtml:
Prof. Christian Happi

Biography

Professor of Molecular Biology and Genomics Director of Institute of Genomics and Global Health Redeemer’s University, Ede, Nigeria

Youtube link : Interview with Professor Christian Happi

Prof. Christian Happi, is a Distinguished Professor of Molecular Biology and Genomics, and Director of Institute of Genomics and Global Health, Redeemer’s University, Ede, Nigeria. He did his postdoctoral fellowship at Harvard University (2000-2003), and worked there as a Research Scientist (2004-2007). He is currently Adjunct Professor in the Department of Immunology and Infectious Diseases, Harvard T H Chan School of Public Health, Boston, USA. Professor Happi used next generation sequencing technology to perform the first sequence of the SARS-CoV-2 in Africa, within 48 hours of receiving a sample of the first case in Nigeria. This seminal work not only provided an insight into the detailed genetic map of the coronavirus in Africa, but also paved the way to the development of new countermeasures including new diagnostics, therapeutics and vaccines. He received the Merle A. Sande Health Leadership Award (2011); the 2019 Human Genome Organization Africa Prize for his seminal work on infectious diseases genomics in Africa; the 2020 Bailey K. Ashford Medal by the American Society of Tropical Medicine and Hygiene. In 2021, he received the Al-Sumait Prize for African Development of the Kuwait Foundation for Advancement in Sciences. He is a Fellow of the Nigerian Academy of Sciences, A Fellow of Academy of Medical Specialties of Nigeria, A Fellow of the African Academy of Sciences, and he is a member of the US National Academy of Medicine.

Go back

2025/speaker-05.name: Prof. Benjamin Aribisala
2025/speaker-05.designation: Department of Computer Science Lagos State University, Nigeria
2025/speaker-05.bodyHtml:
Prof. Benjamin Aribisala

Biography

Department of Computer Science Lagos State University, Nigeria

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Prof. Aribisala Benjamin Seguna

Professor Aribisala Benjamin Segun is a Professor of Computer Science and a Fulbright Scholar. He received his Ph.D. degree in Computer Science from the University of Birmingham, U.K. He became a Professor of computer science in 2013. He has over 29 years of work experience as an academic staff and he has served at several leadership positions in the University, such as the Head of the Department of Computer Science, the Dean of the Faculty of Science, the Director of ICT, a member of the Governing Council in Lagos State University and Vice Chancellor of Oduduwa University. He has worked as an academic staff in the UK, USA and Nigeria. For example, Newcastle University (England, U.K.), the University of Edinburgh (Scotland, U.K.) and The University of Chicago (Illinois, USA) as a Fulbright Visiting Professor of Computer Science. He is currently a Professor of computer science at Lagos State University and the Director of the center of excellence for sargassum research at Lagos State University. In June 2024, he was appointed by the Governor of Lagos State, His Excellency, Mr. Babajide Sanwo-Olu as a member of the Lagos State Research and Innovation Council. His research interest cuts across data science, medical image analysis, machine learning, deep learning and artificial intelligence. His research focuses on improving medical diagnosis, quality of life, and life expectancy. According to google scholar, and as at 19th June 2024, Professor Aribisala has been cited 8,546 times at more than 50 countries. His h-index is 35 and his i10-index is 61. He has 4 publications in Nature and he has given presentations at 6 continents, covering the UK, USA, China, Canada, Jordan, Germany, Nigeria, South Africa, Ghana, Tanzania, Kenya, Portugal and so on. He has won many international awards, e.g. Young Investigators award from the Society for Brain Mapping and Therapeutics, Los Angeles USA in 2015. In 2017, he won the Best Paper Award at the SCI-GAIA European Organisation Conference, Pretoria, South Africa. He won the prestigious Fulbright scholarship in 2022 and that gave him the opportunity to visit the University of Chicago where he conducted research on breast cancer and machine learning. He is a member of many professional bodies, for example, the Computer Professional Registration Council of Nigeria, British Computer Society, IEEE, Organization of Human Brain Mapping and International Society for Magnetic Resonance in Medicine. He is a Fellow of the Nigerian Computer Society.

Go back

2025/speaker-06.name: Dr. Henrietta Mensah Brown
2025/speaker-06.designation: Postdoctoral Fellow West African Centre for Cell Biology of Infectious Pathogens University of Ghana, Ghana
2025/speaker-06.bodyHtml:
Dr. Henrietta Mensah Brown

Biography

Postdoctoral Fellow West African Centre for Cell Biology of Infectious Pathogens University of Ghana, Ghana

Dr. Henrietta Mensah-Brown holds a Bachelor’s degree from Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology, a Masters’ in Biochemistry and a PhD in molecular biology of infectious diseases from the University of Ghana. Her research areas are malaria biology and immunology, focusing on the pathogenesis of Plasmodium falciparum malaria in children. One aspect of her work is aimed at gaining a better understanding of the mechanisms used by Plasmodium falciparum for invasion of red blood cells. Another aspect is focused on investigating immune responses to proteins used in red cell invasion and the targets of invasion inhibitory antibodies. As a TIBA out of Africa postdoctoral fellow, her work focused on Plasmodium falciparum erythrocyte membrane protein 1 (PfEMP-1) which has been implicated in the pathogenesis of severe malaria. Dr. Mensah-Brown is currently an APTI (African Postdoctoral Intiative) fellow at the National Institutes of Health, where she is continuing research to understand severe malaria pathogenesis in children.

Go back

2025/speaker-07.name: Dr. Ammah Benneh
2025/speaker-07.designation: Consultant Haematologist Head of Haemotology University of Ghana Medical School, Ghana
2025/speaker-07.bodyHtml:
Dr. Ammah Benneh

Biography

Consultant Haematologist Head of Haemotology University of Ghana Medical School, Ghana

Youtube link : Dr. Amma Anima Benneh-Akwasi Kuma

Dr. Amma Anima Benneh-Akwasi Kuma is a highly accomplished haematologist, educator, and researcher with extensive expertise in Haematology with special interest in haematological malignancies, sickle cell disease and haemphilia. She is the Head of the Department of Haematology at the University of Ghana Medical School, a Coonsultant Haematologist at Korle-Bu Teaching Hospital and the Deputy Director for Academic and Social Affairs at the West African Genetic Medicine Centre. She also serves as the Director of the Ghana Institute of Clinical Genetics, overseeing adult sickle cell disease management. Dr. Benneh-Akwasi Kuma is a Fellow of both the Ghana College of Physicians and Surgeons and the West African College of Physicians, where she actively contributes to postgraduate medical training and examination. She has held key leadership roles, including Vice President and Past President of the Ghana Society of Haematology, Faculty Secretary for the Faculty of Laboratory Medicine at the West African College of Physicians, and a member of the Technical Working Group for the National Sickle Cell Disease Strategy. Her research focuses on haematological disorders, sickle cell disease, chronic myeloid leukaemia, and blood transfusion medicine, with significant contributions to international collaborations such as SickleGenAfrica, BLOODSAFE, and the Ghana Sickle Pan- African Research Consortium (SPARCO). She has served as a and Co-Investigator on multiple high-impact studies and has received international recognition, including the International Chronic Myeloid Leukaemia Foundation Award for overcoming challenges in treating CML. A dedicated medical educator, she has led numerous haematology training programs, mentored postgraduate students, and contributed to curriculum development. She is an Examiner for the Ghana College of Physicians and Surgeons and the West African College of Physicians, as well as a Member of the University of Ghana Academic Board and College of Health Sciences Academic Board. Dr. Benneh-Akwasi Kuma’s work continues to shape haematology practice, research, and education in Ghana and across West Africa.

Go back

2025/speaker-08.name: Dr. Eneyi Kpokiri
2025/speaker-08.designation: Assistant Professor London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, UK
2025/speaker-08.bodyHtml:
Dr. Eneyi Kpokiri

Biography

Assistant Professor London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, UK

Youtube link : Dr. Eneyi Kpokiri

Dr. Eneyi Kpokiri is a Clinical Pharmacist and an Assistant Professor at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, London, United Kingdom. Her research focuses on identifying and implementing social innovations in health with the use of community-engaged, participatory methods including crowdsourcing open calls and Designathons in low and middle income countries. She has conducted several global crowdsourcing open calls, in-person/digital hackathons and other public engagement methods for infectious disease related global health research projects. She has co-led the development of several WHO/TDR Practical Guides on topics including Public Engagement and Crowdfunding, Monitoring and Evaluation of Social innovations in health and Research Mentorship in LMICs. She is passionate about advancing careers for women in science and is currently working on a WHO practical guide on gender equity and inclusivity in research mentorship.

Go back

2025/speaker-09.name: Dr. Oyesola Ojewunmi
2025/speaker-09.designation: Senior Research Fellow in Genomics, Precision Healthcare University Research Institute (PHURI), Queen Mary University of London (QMUL), UK
2025/speaker-09.bodyHtml:
Dr. Oyesola Ojewunmi

Biography

Senior Research Fellow in Genomics, Precision Healthcare University Research Institute (PHURI), Queen Mary University of London (QMUL), UK

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Dr. Oyesola Ojewunmi

Dr. Oyesola Ojewunmi is a Senior Research Fellow in Genomics at the Precision Healthcare University Research Institute (PHURI), Queen Mary University of London (QMUL), London, UK. His current work uses advanced methods to address genetic heterogeneity in genome-wide association meta-analysis of diverse populations and fine-mapping with emphasis on African ancestry.

His research also focuses on sickle cell disease, investigating genotype–phenotype correlations that integrate multi-omics datasets to unravel markers and pathways with biological and clinical relevance. Prior to this role, Dr. Ojewunmi worked as a Research Fellow in Genetic Epidemiology at the London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine, London, UK.

During his postdoctoral fellowship at King’s College London, Dr. Ojewunmi led a ground-breaking genome-wide association study of fetal haemoglobin in Nigerian patients with sickle cell disease. His exceptional contributions in this role resulted in well-deserved awards and distinctions.

Dr Ojewunmi earned a BSc in Biochemistry from Olabisi Onabanjo University in Nigeria. He obtained an MSc and PhD in Biochemistry with Distinction from the University of Lagos, Nigeria.

Oyesola’s research expertise encompasses various skills crucial for advancing genetic studies, from hands-on wet lab-based experiments to statistical-genetic analyses. His commitment to the academic community is evident through his active involvement in teaching, where he imparts knowledge and guidance to undergraduate and postgraduate students, reflecting his dedication to training the next generation of researchers.

He is also committed to developing young African scientists’ capacity for genomics and data science. He has published more than 20 research articles in peer-reviewed journals and is a Review Editor for Frontiers in Haematology.

Go back

2025/speaker-10.name: Dr. Joseph Tatuene
2025/speaker-10.designation: Neurologist and Neuroscientist; Wellcome Trust Early Career Fellow, Wolfson Centre for Prevention of Stroke and Dementia, University of Oxford
2025/speaker-10.bodyHtml:
Dr. Joseph Tatuene

Biography

Neurologist and Neuroscientist; Wellcome Trust Early Career Fellow, Wolfson Centre for Prevention of Stroke and Dementia, University of Oxford

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Dr Joseph Kamtchum

Dr Joseph Kamtchum is a Neurologist and Neuroscientist with expertise in Neuroepidemiology, Neurovascular Ultrasound, and Translational Stroke Research. He is currently a Wellcome Trust Early Career Fellow at the University of Oxford Wolfson Centre for Prevention of Stroke and Dementia, working to establish the Oxford-Cameroon Atherosclerosis and Stroke Study (OxCASS).

The overarching goal of the project is to investigate ethnic differences in stroke aetiology in the United Kingdom and clarify stroke epidemiology and mechanisms in Cameroon, Central Africa. His ultimate goal is to establish an atherosclerosis and stroke program in Africa with the mission of (1) generating evidence to inform local stroke policies and guidelines, (2) training the next generations of stroke clinician-scientists, (3) enabling the inclusion of African populations into international stroke research initiatives, and (4) delivering quality stroke care for all.

Go back

2025/speaker-11.name: Mr. Francis Kalule
2025/speaker-11.designation: Diagnostic Regional Technical Manager for Sub-Saharan Africa, Zoetis
2025/speaker-11.bodyHtml:
Mr. Francis Kalule

Biography

Diagnostic Regional Technical Manager for Sub-Saharan Africa, Zoetis

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Mr Francis Kalule

Francis Kalule is a distinguished biomedical laboratory technologist with over a decade of excellence in human diagnostic healthcare and seven years of expertise in veterinary diagnostics. As a specialist in molecular biology and public health, Francis has amassed substantial research experience in both human and animal health. His dedication to advancing diagnostic tools is driven by a passion for improving disease management, combating antimicrobial resistance, and championing the One Health approach.

In his professional capacity, Francis serves as the Diagnostic Regional Technical Manager for Sub-Saharan Africa at Zoetis, a global leader in animal health. He excels at designing, leading, and implementing a diverse range of sustainable growth and realignment initiatives in partnership with various veterinary diagnostic collaborators. His efforts have significantly enhanced diagnostic networks, improved training for laboratory professionals, and established robust diagnostic infrastructures that support the animal health industry across Sub-Saharan Africa.

Beyond his professional role, Francis is actively involved in various impactful initiatives across the African continent. He serves with the Mentorship Guild of the Tony Elumelu Foundation, guiding aspiring African entrepreneurs towards healthcare business success. He is also a member of the Leaders of Africa Institute Alumni Community and the Biochemistry and Biotechnology Professionals Society of Kenya.

Additionally, he is a sought-after speaker at various international conferences, where he promotes the role of veterinary diagnostics in addressing emerging global health challenges. His presentations have inspired many in the field to adopt innovative diagnostic solutions and collaborative approaches to health management.

Go back

2025/speaker-12.name: Mr. Alex Kandie
2025/speaker-12.designation: Senior Communication Specialist, Science for Africa Foundation
2025/speaker-12.bodyHtml:
Mr. Alex Kandie

Biography

Senior Communication Specialist, Science for Africa Foundation

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Mr Alex Kandie

Alex Kandie is a seasoned communication expert with over a decade of experience translating complex ideas into compelling narratives that spark action. Currently holding the role of Senior Communication Officer at the Science for Africa Foundation, he is passionate about making science accessible, relatable, and impactful—especially in Africa.

His career spans high-level communication roles across leading development organisations including Gatsby Africa, Kenya Markets Trust, and the Centre for Health Solutions–Kenya. Through these roles, Alex has led the design and execution of communication strategies, developed engaging multimedia content, supported scientists across agriculture, health, and climate change sectors, and amplified voices driving African-led innovation.

Beyond strategy, Alex is a storyteller at heart. He has authored a teen novella, edited a careers encyclopaedia for youth, and led the communication of milestone research including Kenya’s national TB prevalence survey. He also played a key editorial role in the publication of BOMA: Behind the Grass Curtain, a historical adventure novel.

From live storytelling events and literary festivals to policy dialogues and digital campaigns, Alex brings a versatile, audience-centred approach to science communication. His mission is to close the gap between evidence and action, and to inspire a new generation of African scientists and storytellers to do the same.

When he’s not championing research, Alex enjoys photography, cycling, exploring nature, and building creative projects that blend design with impact.

Go back

2025/speaker-13.name: Ms. Hellen Shikanda
2025/speaker-13.designation: Health and Science Journalist, Nation Media Group
2025/speaker-13.bodyHtml:
Ms. Hellen Shikanda

Biography

Health and Science Journalist, Nation Media Group

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Ms Hellen Shikanda

Ms Shikanda holds a Bachelor’s degree in Communication and Journalism from Moi University in Kenya. She received postgraduate training in Multimedia Journalism from the Graduate School of Media, Aga Khan University.

She has been a reporter for almost six years now, specialising in producing multimedia content in health and science for the Nation Media Group. She is a fact-checker, a leader, and a climate change enthusiast. Ms Shikanda is an alumna of the Bettina Fund Mentorship Program, the Oxford Climate Journalism Network, Africa Resilience Network and the Africa Academy of Open-Source Investigation.

She has received a number of international fellowships such as the Women in News fellowship, the Climate Tracker COP27 fellowship and the National Press Foundation fellowship on Covering Rare Diseases, Internews Health Journalism Network fellowship among others. She has received a number of media awards and recognition both locally and internationally. She was the first journalist from an African-based media house to receive the 2024 AACR June L. Biedler Award for Cancer Journalism.

Go back

2025/speaker-14.name: Mr. Paballo Chauke
2025/speaker-14.designation: Training Coordinator, Wellcome Sanger Institute; Chair, H3Africa Education Working Group
2025/speaker-14.bodyHtml:
Mr. Paballo Chauke

Biography

Training Coordinator, Wellcome Sanger Institute; Chair, H3Africa Education Working Group

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Mr Paballo Chauke

Paballo Chauke is a Training Coordinator at Wellcome Sanger Institute, and Chair of the H3Africa Education Working Group. An award-winning science communicator and educator, he has trained over 10,000 scientists globally.

A prolific writer and speaker, Paballo is a Mail & Guardian columnist and founder of Conversations with Chauke. He is a malaria champion with the Zero Malaria campaign and has presented to over 60,000 learners at SciFest Africa in South Africa. His work blends science, education, and advocacy across Africa and beyond.

He has helped manage science communication for H3Africa, DS-I Africa and the African Society of Human Genetics, just to name a few. Paballo believes science is for all and that scientists need to embrace science communication as part of their vocation.

Go back

2025/speaker-15.name: Dr. Patience Kiyuka
2025/speaker-15.designation: Principal Research Scientist, Kenya Medical Research Institute (KEMRI)
2025/speaker-15.bodyHtml:
Dr. Patience Kiyuka

Biography

Principal Research Scientist, Kenya Medical Research Institute (KEMRI)

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Dr Patience Kiyuka

Dr Patience Kiyuka is a Principal Research Scientist at the Kenya Medical Research Institute (KEMRI). Patience is the winner of the 2022 Eric and Wendy Schmidt Award for Excellence in Science Communication and a former fellow at Vertex Pharmaceuticals in Oxford, UK.

At the KEMRI–Centre for Geographic Medicine Research–Coast, she leads innovative public health initiatives focused on combating infectious diseases through advanced diagnostics and surveillance systems. Her work spans three critical areas: firstly, she coordinates genomic surveillance of high-priority pathogens, including dengue, chikungunya, drug-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Mycobacterium tuberculosis across Africa and Southeast Asia, generating essential data to track climate- and resistance-driven transmission patterns.

Secondly, she directs febrile illness monitoring programs that employ cutting-edge molecular diagnostics to identify causative pathogens and improve treatment protocols. Thirdly, her malaria research examines transmission dynamics in vulnerable populations, particularly investigating gametocyte carriage in pregnant women receiving preventive therapy while overseeing hospital-based surveillance of diagnostic accuracy and antimalarial resistance markers.

Together, these efforts aim to translate scientific insights into effective disease control strategies that address pressing public health challenges in resource-limited settings.

Go back

2025/speaker-16.name: Dr. Carol Ibe
2025/speaker-16.designation: Founder, JR Biotek Foundation; Research Manager, Cambridge Global Challenges Interdisciplinary Research Centre, University of Cambridge
2025/speaker-16.bodyHtml:
Dr. Carol Ibe

Biography

Founder, JR Biotek Foundation; Research Manager, Cambridge Global Challenges Interdisciplinary Research Centre, University of Cambridge

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Dr Carol Ibe

Dr Carol Ibe is a molecular plant scientist, visionary leader, and Founder of the JR Biotek Foundation, a nonprofit dedicated to empowering African scientists and farmers to co-create climate-resilient solutions for food security. She also serves as Research Manager at the Cambridge Global Challenges Interdisciplinary Research Centre at the University of Cambridge.

A Gates Cambridge Scholar with academic training from Cambridge, Oxford, and Georgetown, Carol has led pioneering initiatives that unite science, indigenous knowledge, and creative public engagement. Her leadership has trained over 260 African crop scientists in person and reached more than 10,000 virtually across 19+ countries, while fostering Africa-led innovations that support African smallholder farmers.

Through projects like the Agri-Innovation & Impact Project (AGRIIP) and the Roots of Resilience initiative (both initiatives of the JR Biotek Foundation), she is advancing equitable partnerships, amplifying African perspectives, and reimagining agriculture as a pathway to resilience and human flourishing.

Go back

2025/speaker-17.name: Prof. Nicola Mulder
2025/speaker-17.designation: Head, Computational Biology Division, University of Cape Town; Principal Investigator, DS-I Africa eLwazi Open Data Science Platform
2025/speaker-17.bodyHtml:
Prof. Nicola Mulder

Biography

Head, Computational Biology Division, University of Cape Town; Principal Investigator, DS-I Africa eLwazi Open Data Science Platform

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Prof. Nicola Mulder

Prof Mulder heads the Computational Biology Division at the University of Cape Town (UCT). She is PI of the DS-I Africa eLwazi Open Data Science Platform and co-leads a Sickle Cell Disease Data Coordinating Centre and a Wellcome Trust Discovery Data Integration Platform at UCT. She led H3ABioNet, a Pan African Bioinformatics Network which developed bioinformatics capacity to enable genomic data analysis for more than a decade.

Her research focuses on genetic determinants of susceptibility to disease, African genome variation, and microbial genomics and infectious diseases. Her group provides bioinformatics services and training and develops new algorithms and resources for African genomics. Prof Mulder is actively involved in capacity development in bioinformatics and genomics. She also sits on a number of international scientific advisory boards and is currently the interim Director for the African Bioinformatics Institute.

Go back

2025/speaker-18.name: Prof. Thumbi Mwangi
2025/speaker-18.designation: Associate Professor of Epidemiology, Washington State University’s Paul G. Allen School for Global Health; Co-founder and Co-director, Center for Epidemiological Modelling and Analysis (CEMA), University of Nairobi
2025/speaker-18.bodyHtml:
Prof. Thumbi Mwangi

Biography

Associate Professor of Epidemiology, Washington State University’s Paul G. Allen School for Global Health; Co-founder and Co-director, Center for Epidemiological Modelling and Analysis (CEMA), University of Nairobi

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Prof. Thumbi Mwangi

Thumbi Mwangi is an Associate Professor of Epidemiology at Washington State University’s Paul G. Allen School for Global Health, and Co-founder and Co-director of the Center for Epidemiological Modelling and Analysis (CEMA) at the University of Nairobi. Based in Kenya, he leads a research team using applied epidemiological and economic modelling to inform public health policies and control/elimination programs of epidemic and endemic diseases in Africa.

He holds a degree in Veterinary Medicine and Surgery, an MSc in Genetics and Animal Breeding from the University of Nairobi, and a Ph.D. in Infectious Disease Epidemiology from the University of Edinburgh. He has previously served as the Chair of the Kenya COVID-19 Technical Committee on Modelling, the Co-chair of the WHO/FAO/WOAH United Against Rabies Working Group, a Lancet Commissioner on strengthening the use of epidemiological modelling of emerging and pandemic infectious diseases, and is currently an Affiliate Fellow of the African Academy of Sciences.

Go back

2025/speaker-19.name: Assoc. Prof. Peter Quashie
2025/speaker-19.designation: Deputy Director of Research, West African Centre for Cell Biology of Infectious Pathogens (WACCBIP), University of Ghana; Associate Professor of Molecular Virology
2025/speaker-19.bodyHtml:
Assoc. Prof. Peter Quashie

Biography

Deputy Director of Research, West African Centre for Cell Biology of Infectious Pathogens (WACCBIP), University of Ghana; Associate Professor of Molecular Virology

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Assoc. Prof. Peter Quashie

Prof. Peter Kojo Quashie is Deputy Director of Research at the West African Centre for Cell Biology of Infectious Pathogens (WACCBIP), University of Ghana, and an Associate Professor of Molecular Virology. His research focuses on HIV, SARS-CoV-2, and epidemic-prone RNA viruses, emphasizing host–virus interactions and antiviral therapeutics. He has authored over 65 peer-reviewed publications in leading journals such as Journal of Virology, Science, Nature Communications, Cell Reports, and BMC Medicine—many as senior or lead author.

He leads several international collaborations, including serving as Ghana lead for the SPIL-OVA project under the Africa Pandemic Science Collaborative’s EPSILON network, and Principal Investigator for the Ghana site of the Global Immunology and Immune Sequencing for Epidemic Response (GIISER) program, funded by the Gates Foundation. His team is pioneering surveillance tools to detect zoonotic viruses in mosquitoes and bats.

In HIV research, he launched his laboratory in 2019 with support from a Crick African Network award. He established the WACCBIP long-term HIV Infection CoHort (WHICH), tracking up to 1,000 patients to study determinants of treatment success, failure, and viral control. He is Ghana PI for 1+2=Cure, an AIDSFonds-funded HIV cure project leveraging samples from HIV-1/2 coinfected participants. He has recently been awarded an AfOx Fellowship to implement advanced HIV sequencing pipelines in Ghana.

Prof. Quashie holds Honours and Master’s degrees in Cell and Molecular Biology from Concordia University and a PhD in Experimental Medicine from McGill University, where he trained under the late Prof. Mark Wainberg. His PhD studies, supported by a doctoral scholarship jointly funded by the Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR) and the Canadian Association for HIV Researchers (CAHR), produced 33 publications, including seminal papers on HIV integrase resistance pathways (R263K and G118R) to dolutegravir—findings that continue to inform global treatment guidelines.

He completed postdoctoral training in Structural Virology at the University of Toronto under Prof. Jeffrey E. Lee, funded by a CIHR Banting Fellowship. There, he specialized in monoclonal antibody development and host–virus protein interactions.

At WACCBIP, Quashie played a central role in Ghana’s COVID-19 response, coordinating research on serosurveillance, sequencing, immunopathology, and therapeutics. His group published landmark studies linking Ghana’s lower COVID-19 severity to malaria endemicity.

As Deputy Director, he drives WACCBIP’s research strategy, postdoctoral development, and stakeholder engagement. He also leads the Climate Health Resilience activity within the futures lab of the University of Ghana’s arm of the Africa Climate Collaborative, advancing research on climate impacts on disease trends.

Prof. Quashie represents WACCBIP in national policy engagements, including with Ghana’s National Vaccine Institute (NVI). He joined a 2022 delegation to Cuba to study COVID-19 vaccines and in 2025 is contributing to shaping Ghana’s vaccine R&D roadmap at the World Vaccine Congress.

He has supervised four postdoctoral fellows, more than ten PhD students (four graduated), ten Master’s students (three graduated), and numerous undergraduates and interns. A global citizen with experience in Ghana, Botswana, Canada, and extended stays in the UK, his trainees come from across Africa and beyond.

Prof. Peter Kojo Quashie’s social media handles are:
LinkedIn: www.linkedin.com/in/peter-kojo-quashie-phd-19951723
X (Twitter): @DrPKQuashie
ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0003-4114-5460

2025/speaker-20.name: Dr. Tabitha Mwangi
2025/speaker-20.designation: Programme Director, Mastercard Foundation Scholars and Collaborative Program, University of Cambridge
2025/speaker-20.bodyHtml:
Dr. Tabitha Mwangi

Biography

Programme Director, Mastercard Foundation Scholars and Collaborative Program, University of Cambridge

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Dr. Tabitha Mwangi

Tabitha Mwangi is programme director at the Mastercard Foundation Scholars and Collaborative Program at the University of Cambridge. Prior to this, she was Cambridge-Africa Programme Manager. She has been Senior Lecturer in Public Health at Anglia Ruskin University, UK (2017–2020) and Pwani University in Kilifi, Kenya (2013–2015).

Tabitha started her higher education at the University of Nairobi in Kenya, earning a Bachelor’s in Veterinary Medicine and an MSc in Veterinary Epidemiology. She worked as a research scientist for 10 years at the Kenya Medical Research Institute–Wellcome Trust Research Program in Kilifi where she completed her PhD in malaria epidemiology.

Tabitha’s other love is writing. As a free-lance science writer, she has written articles for the Daily Nation, a leading national paper in Kenya, Msafiri (Kenya Airways flight magazine), The Conversation, and her blog (http://healthkenya.co.ke). Tabitha has also published a book profiling a number of prominent scientists from the African continent titled, 12 Remarkable Africa Life Scientists (https://amzn.eu/d/cnEc1ce).

Go back

2025/speaker-21.name: Dr. ThankGod Ebenezer
2025/speaker-21.designation: Global Engagement Manager for EMBL-EBI Data Services, EMBL-European Bioinformatics Institute (EMBL-EBI), UK
2025/speaker-21.bodyHtml:
Dr. ThankGod Ebenezer

Biography

Global Engagement Manager for EMBL-EBI Data Services, EMBL-European Bioinformatics Institute (EMBL-EBI), UK

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Dr. ThankGod Echezona Ebenezer

Dr. ThankGod Echezona Ebenezer is the Global Engagement Manager for EMBL-EBI Data Services at the EMBL-European Bioinformatics Institute (EMBL-EBI), UK, where his role focuses on exploring new approaches, data, metadata, functions, services, and potential uses of open data in low-resource settings. Prior to taking up this role, ThankGod was a Bioinformatician at Qbio.AI and University of Cambridge School of Clinical Medicine where his role focused on addressing drug discovery challenges using AI and developing computational pipelines for early cancer detection using circulating tumour DNA methylation markers and machine learning techniques respectively.

Prior to joining Qbio.AI and Cambridge, Dr Ebenezer was previously a Bioinformatician at EMBL-EBI, within the UniProt team, where he developed computational pipelines for automatic functional annotation of proteins using evolutionary models and software engineering best practices. Dr Ebenezer studied for his PhD degree in Biochemistry at the University of Cambridge, UK, where he worked on the genome of Euglena gracilis. He has a MSc in Environmental Toxicology and Pollution Management from the University of Lagos, Nigeria, and a BSc in Parasitology and Entomology from Nnamdi Azikiwe University, Awka, Nigeria.

Dr Ebenezer is involved in fostering dynamic international and regional collaborations in science. Dr Ebenezer founded the African BioGenome Project (AfricaBP) in 2021. Since 2023 AfricaBP has trained over 800 African researchers in genomics, bioinformatics, sampling, ethics, legal, and social implications of genomics, and has also locally sequenced the genomes of several endemic African plant and animal species.

Go back

2025/speaker-22.name: Dr. Sammy Wambua
2025/speaker-22.designation: Head, Pwani University Biosciences Research Centre (PUBReC), Kilifi, Kenya
2025/speaker-22.bodyHtml:
Dr. Sammy Wambua

Biography

Head, Pwani University Biosciences Research Centre (PUBReC), Kilifi, Kenya

Youtube Interview link: Interview with Dr. Sammy Wambua

Sammy Wambua is a conservation genomics researcher and Head of the Pwani University Biosciences Research Centre (PUBReC) in Kilifi, Kenya. He leads the Wildlife Genomics Lab, using metagenomics (eDNA) and population genomics to study tropical species of conservation concern—from corals and sea turtles to fish, bees, snakes, elephants, and primates. He is also the founding chair of the Research & Conservation Support Society (RECOURSE), a reader society that provides technical, logistical, and administrative support to scientists and conservation practitioners.

Sammy is passionate about mentoring students and building local capacity. He designs and teaches hands-on courses, supervises early-career researchers, and partners across and outside Africa to equip teams with the skills, tools, and collaborative habits that make science work in real-world settings.

Go back
